text: During the total laparoscopic hysterectomy, the surgeon performs adhesiolysis to separate the densely adherent uterus from the intestines. The left pararectal space is entered, and the gut is carefully dissected away from the uterus. The right ovarian cysts are opened and drained, and the cysts, which are adherent to the pelvic side wall and intestines, are released.

The surgeon releases the sigmoid colon, which is densely adherent to the uterus and the right utero sacral ligament. The right uterine artery is dissected and ligated at its origin from the internal iliac artery, followed by the ligation of the left uterine artery at its origin, aiming to devascularize the uterus for the hysterectomy.
The surgeon dissects the sigmoid colon away from the uterus, entering the right pararectal space to completely release the colon from its dense adhesions to the uterine tissue.

The surgeon coagulates and cuts the left round ligament, followed by the dissection of the left ureter. The left infundibulopelvic ligament is then coagulated and cut. The right ureter is identified, and the right infundibulopelvic ligament is coagulated and cut.

The surgeon coagulates and cuts the right round ligament. The peritoneum over the bladder is incised, allowing the bladder to be separated from the cervix. The ascending branch of the right uterine artery is isolated, coagulated, and cut. The vaginal vault is opened anteriorly using a co-cup.

A circumferential incision is made, completely detaching the uterus from the cervix. The uterus is then removed vaginally. The surgeon closes the vaginal vault laparoscopically using a continuous suture technique.
The final view of the pelvis shows the surgical field after the completion of the hysterectomy, with the uterus removed and the vaginal vault closed.
